threats.title: Lock Duration Bypass via Flawed Validation in setLockDuration
threats.hypothesis_description: The setLockDuration function contains a flawed validation check that allows users to bypass their lock duration and withdraw tokens immediately.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function validates the new duration by checking if block.timestamp + _duration < old_unlockTime. However, it sets the new unlock time to lastLockTime + _duration. If lastLockTime is sufficiently in the past, the new unlock time can be set to a timestamp that has already passed, allowing immediate withdrawal.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation uses block.timestamp but assignment uses lastLockTime

preconditions: User has an existing locked position
Sufficient time has passed since lastLockTime such that lastLockTime + new_duration <= block.timestamp

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Mismatched time bases in validation vs assignment
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that the final unlockTime is >= block.timestamp

analysis_questions: Can a user completely bypass a long lock duration?
Does this allow premature extraction of lockdrop rewards?
validation_requirements: Create a test where a user locks for 100 days, waits 50 days, and sets duration to 10 days
Verify if the user can unlock immediately

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker locks tokens for maximum duration to receive maximum lockdrop NFT rewards. Later, attacker calls setLockDuration with a small value, bypassing the remaining lock time and withdrawing principal while keeping unearned rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of protocol TVL and unearned distribution of lockdrop rewards

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for setLockDuration with time advancement

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block (requires waiting some time after initial lock)

threats.title: Permanent Fund Freezing via lockOnBehalf Griefing
threats.hypothesis_description: The lockOnBehalf function allows any user to reset the lock duration of any other user's tokens, permanently freezing their funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _lock function unconditionally updates unlockTime to block.timestamp + _lockDuration for the _lockRecipient. Since lockOnBehalf allows an attacker to specify any recipient and lock 0 tokens (or 1 wei), they can repeatedly extend a victim's lock time at near-zero cost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unconditional update of victim's unlockTime without consent or minimum amount

preconditions: Victim has an existing locked position with an unlockTime in the future

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that msg.sender == _lockRecipient or that new unlockTime >= old unlockTime

analysis_questions: Can an attacker lock 0 tokens for a victim?
Does this successfully extend the victim's lock duration?
validation_requirements: Test lockOnBehalf with 0 quantity for an ERC20 token
Verify the victim's unlockTime is extended

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker monitors the blockchain for users whose lock durations are about to expire. The attacker calls lockOnBehalf with 0 tokens for the victim, resetting their unlockTime to the future and preventing withdrawal.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for lockOnBehalf extending existing locks

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as unexpected lock extensions

threats.title: Protocol Insolvency with Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract does not correctly account for fee-on-transfer tokens, leading to protocol insolvency.
threats.investigation_rationale: When locking tokens, the contract increments the user's locked quantity by the requested _quantity rather than the actual amount received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, this results in the contract owing more tokens than it holds.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured by the admin
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation explicitly states support for fee-on-transfer tokens under 'Weird ERC20 Support'.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State updated with _quantity instead of balance delta after transferFrom

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured and active
Users lock and unlock this token

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No measurement of actual tokens received

analysis_questions: Does the protocol intend to support fee-on-transfer tokens?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock fee-on-transfer token
Lock tokens and verify contract balance is less than total locked quantity

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users lock a fee-on-transfer token. The contract records the full amount but receives less. When users attempt to unlock, the last users will fail due to insufficient contract balance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of principal for late-withdrawing users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the specific token

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Mismatch between contract balance and total locked quantity

threats.title: Free Token Locking via Unchecked ERC20 Return Values
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract uses raw ERC20 transferFrom calls without checking the return value, allowing free token locking for certain tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Some ERC20 tokens (like ZRX) return false on failure instead of reverting. The _lock function does not check the return value of transferFrom. An attacker can call lock with a token that returns false, bypassing the transfer but still receiving locked balance.
deployment_assumptions: A token that returns false on failure is configured
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation explicitly states support for 'missing return values' under 'Weird ERC20 Support'.
preconditions: A token that returns false on failure instead of reverting is configured

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require() on the return value

analysis_questions: Can an attacker lock tokens without actually transferring them?
Can they later unlock and drain the contract?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock token that returns false on failure
Attempt to lock without sufficient balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker approves the contract but has 0 balance. Attacker calls lock(). transferFrom returns false but execution continues. Attacker is credited with locked tokens. Attacker later unlocks, draining real tokens from the contract.
prerequisites: Specific token behavior (returns false on failure)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of the specific token from the contract
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the specific token

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests with non-standard ERC20 tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as failed transfers followed by successful locks

threats.title: Multisig Governance Bypass via Disapprove-then-Approve Vote Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: Price feed operators can inflate vote counts by disapproving and then approving the same proposal.
threats.investigation_rationale: disapproveUSDPrice records the vote in disapprovals[msg.sender], but approveUSDPrice only checks approvals[msg.sender]. A malicious operator can call disapprove and then approve, incrementing both disapprovalsCount and approvalsCount, bypassing the intended multisig thresholds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing cross-mapping check between approvals and disapprovals

preconditions: An active USD update proposal exists

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: approveUSDPrice does not check if msg.sender has already disapproved

analysis_questions: Can a single operator cast both an approve and disapprove vote?
Does this break the multisig security model?
validation_requirements: Create a proposal
Have an operator call disapproveUSDPrice then approveUSDPrice
Verify both counts incremented

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised price feed operator disapproves a proposal to inflate the disapprovalsCount, then approves it to inflate the approvalsCount, manipulating the outcome of the governance process.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Manipulation of USD prices, leading to incorrect reward distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin intervention to fix prices

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for operators changing votes or voting multiple times

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as multiple votes from the same address

threats.title: Denial of Service in getLockedWeightedValue for Tokens with >18 Decimals
threats.hypothesis_description: Tokens with more than 18 decimals will cause getLockedWeightedValue to revert, breaking reward calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The calculation 10 ** (18 - decimals) will underflow and revert in Solidity 0.8.x if decimals > 18. This function is used externally to calculate rewards, meaning a single >18 decimal token will DoS the reward system for all users holding it.
deployment_assumptions: A token with >18 decimals is configured
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation explicitly states support for tokens with 'very high (>18) decimals'.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Subtraction 18 - decimals without checking if decimals <= 18

preconditions: A token with >18 decimals is configured
A user has locked this token

analysis_questions: Does the protocol intend to support >18 decimal tokens?
Will this revert block critical external operations?
validation_requirements: Configure a token with 24 decimals
Lock the token and call getLockedWeightedValue

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin configures a token with 24 decimals (e.g., NEAR). Users lock the token. Any external contract calling getLockedWeightedValue for these users will revert, preventing them from claiming rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin must remove or reconfigure the token

threats.title: Loss of NFT Remainder Value when Locking Outside Lockdrop Period
threats.hypothesis_description: Users lose their accumulated NFT remainder value if they lock tokens outside of an active lockdrop period.
threats.investigation_rationale: The remainder variable defaults to 0. If a lock occurs outside the lockdrop period, the NFT calculation block is skipped, and lockedToken.remainder is unconditionally overwritten with 0, destroying the user's previously saved remainder.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unconditional overwrite of lockedToken.remainder with uninitialized/zero variable

preconditions: User has a non-zero remainder from a previous lockdrop
User locks tokens outside of an active lockdrop period

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No preservation of existing remainder if lockdrop is inactive

analysis_questions: Is the remainder intended to be preserved between lockdrops?
Does locking outside a lockdrop destroy this value?
validation_requirements: Lock tokens during lockdrop to generate a remainder
Lock tokens after lockdrop ends
Verify remainder is 0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user accumulates a remainder during a lockdrop. After the lockdrop ends, they lock more tokens. Their previously accumulated remainder is permanently deleted, causing a loss of potential future NFT mints.
prerequisites: Active lockdrop followed by inactive period

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of NFT value for users

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for locking outside lockdrop with existing remainder

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as remainder decreasing to 0

threats.title: Unauthorized Modification of Victim's Global Lock Duration via lockOnBehalf
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can forcefully set a victim's global lock duration preference via lockOnBehalf.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a victim has not set a lockDuration (it is 0), _lock defaults it to lockdrop.minLockDuration and saves it to playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration. An attacker calling lockOnBehalf can trigger this, overwriting the victim's implicit preference.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State mutation of playerSettings[_lockRecipient] during lockOnBehalf

preconditions: Attacker calls lockOnBehalf for the victim

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that msg.sender == _lockRecipient before updating playerSettings

analysis_questions: Can an attacker modify a victim's playerSettings?
Does this affect the victim's future locks?
validation_requirements: Call lockOnBehalf for a new user
Verify their playerSettings.lockDuration is updated

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls lockOnBehalf for a new user before they can set their own lock duration. The user's global lock duration is permanently set to the lockdrop minimum, affecting all their future locks unless they explicitly change it.
prerequisites: Victim has not set a lock duration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: User can call setLockDuration to fix it

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for lockOnBehalf modifying recipient settings

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as playerSettings updates from third parties

threats.title: ETH Funds Frozen for Smart Contract Wallets due to transfer() Gas Stipend
threats.hypothesis_description: Smart contract wallets that consume more than 2300 gas in their receive function will have their ETH permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unlock function uses payable(msg.sender).transfer(_quantity) to return ETH. This forwards a fixed stipend of 2300 gas. If the caller is a smart contract wallet (e.g., a multisig or account abstraction wallet) that requires more gas to receive ETH, the transfer will revert, permanently locking their funds.
preconditions: User is a smart contract wallet that consumes > 2300 gas on receive

analysis_questions: Will smart contract wallets be able to withdraw ETH?
Is there any alternative withdrawal path?
validation_requirements: Deploy a smart contract wallet that consumes > 2300 gas on receive
Lock ETH and attempt to unlock
Verify the transaction reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user interacts with the protocol using a multisig wallet (e.g., Gnosis Safe). They lock ETH successfully. When they attempt to unlock, the transaction reverts due to out-of-gas in the receive function, permanently locking their ETH.
prerequisites: User uses a smart contract wallet

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of ETH for affected users

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for ETH withdrawals to smart contracts

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas consumption of standard smart contract wallets
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Reverted unlock transactions with out-of-gas errors
